"""

This report presents a comprehensive financial analysis of AAPL. based on its 2025 fiscal data. The analysis includes:

  • Financial ratio evaluations
  • Stock valuation using the CAPM model
  • Monte Carlo simulations for future stock prices
  • A comparative analysis of intrinsic value per share across different WACC scenarios

The aim is to assess Apple's profitability, liquidity, growth potential, and investment attractiveness from a financial analyst’s perspective.

In [4]:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import linregress
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

else:

1. Apple Stock Price Trend¶

2. CAPM and Cost of Equity  – Apple vs. S&P 500¶






















In this part, we calculate Apple’s Beta using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) framework.


    

  • Beta is derived via linear regression of Apple’s daily returns against the S&P 500 index returns.
    • 

  • AAPL’s Beta measures its systematic risk relative to the overall market.
    • 

  • The resulting Beta value indicates how sensitive Apple’s stock is to market movements:
      

    • Beta > 1 → More volatile than the market
      • 

    • Beta < 1 → Less volatile
      • 

    

    • 





In this case, the calculated Beta ≈ 1.2072, implying:




    

  • Beta = 1.2 → Apple's volatility is about 20% higher than the market.
    • 

  • If the S&P 500 rises 10%, Apple could rise about 12%.
    • 

  • If the market falls, Apple also falls faster → higher systemic risk.
    • 



















In [8]:






# Calculate daily yield
aapl_returns = apple_close.pct_change().dropna()
sp500_returns = sp500_close.pct_change().dropna()
#Combine data into a DataFrame
df = pd.concat([aapl_returns, sp500_returns], axis=1)
df.columns = ['AAPL_Returns', 'SP500_Returns']
df.dropna(inplace=True)
# Calculate Beta using linear regression
slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = linregress(df['SP500_Returns'], df['AAPL_Returns'])
beta = slope
print(f"Beta = {beta:.4f}")

























Beta = 1.2072

























Beta Visualization via Regression Plot¶
The scatter plot below visualizes the relationship between Apple’s returns and the S&P 500’s returns. The red line represents the regression line, whose slope equals Apple’s Beta.


    

  • Each point represents a pair of daily returns (AAPL vs. SP500).
    • 

  • A clear positive slope indicates that Apple’s returns are positively correlated with the market.
    • 

  • The regression line provides a visual confirmation of the Beta value computed earlier.
    • 





This regression plot supports the CAPM-based risk assessment and enhances the understanding of Apple's market sensitivity.




















In [9]:






# Regression Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
plt.scatter(df['SP500_Returns'], df['AAPL_Returns'], alpha=0.5)
plt.plot(df['SP500_Returns'], intercept + beta * df['SP500_Returns'], color='red')
plt.title('Beta Calculation via Regression')
plt.xlabel('S&P500 Returns')
plt.ylabel('AAPL Returns')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()












































In [10]:






import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Asume the outlier is that the APPL return exceed ±10%
threshold = 0.10

# Filter outlier
outliers = df[(df['AAPL_Returns'] > threshold) | (df['AAPL_Returns'] < -threshold)]

# Print date and value of outlier
print("Outliers detected:")
print(outliers)

# Draw scatter plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
plt.scatter(df['SP500_Returns'], df['AAPL_Returns'], alpha=0.5, label='Data points')

# Draw regression
plt.plot(df['SP500_Returns'], intercept + beta * df['SP500_Returns'], color='red', label='Regression line')

# Mark outlier on plot
plt.scatter(outliers['SP500_Returns'], outliers['AAPL_Returns'], color='orange', edgecolor='black', s=100, label='Outliers')

plt.title('AAPL vs SP500 Returns with Outliers Highlighted')
plt.xlabel('SP500 Returns')
plt.ylabel('AAPL Returns')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

























Outliers detected:
            AAPL_Returns  SP500_Returns
Date                                   
2020-03-13      0.119809       0.092871
2020-03-16     -0.128647      -0.119841
2020-03-24      0.100325       0.093828
2020-07-31      0.104689       0.007671
2025-04-09      0.153288       0.095154




































Outlier Explanation:


On April 9, 2025, Apple (AAPL) experienced an unusually high daily return of +15.33%, compared to the S&P 500 increase of +9.51%. This outlier is attributed to the official announcement that the U.S. government would postpone new trade tariffs for 90 days, which significantly eased market concerns over production costs and global supply chain risks, especially benefiting large tech companies like Apple.






















CAPM-Based Cost of Equity (ROE)¶
Using the CAPM model, we calculate Apple’s cost of equity based on data from the Apple 2025 Annual Report Excel file, from U.S. Department of Treasury – Daily Treasury Yield Curve Rates and yfinance:


    

  • Risk-free rate = 0.0269 → 2.69%/year (10-year US Treasury yield - average 10 year start='2015-12-31', end='2025-12-31')
    • 

  • Market return = 13.85% (S&P500 10-year average: start='2015-12-31', end='2025-12-31')
    • 

  • Beta ≈ 1.2 (calculated from historical regression of AAPL vs. S&P 500 returns)
    • 



A return on equity (ROE) of 16.16% per year is the level of return that investors require for investing in Apple stock.


The result (16.16%) represents the expected return required by equity investors, and is used to estimate the cost of equity in valuation models such as WACC or DCF.


















In [11]:






# U.S. Department of Treasury – Daily Treasury Yield Curve Rates
import yfinance as yf
# Download US 10-Year Treasury Yield Data
tnx = yf.download('^TNX', start='2015-12-31', end='2025-12-31')
# Calculate the average risk-free interest rate over the period
Rf = tnx['Close'].mean()/100
print(Rf)

























[*********************100%***********************]  1 of 1 completed












Ticker
^TNX    0.026915
dtype: float64

































In [12]:






# Get Market return from actual data.
# Download S&P 500 data from 2015 to 2025
sp500_df = yf.download('^GSPC', start='2015-12-31', end='2025-12-31')
sp500_close = sp500_df['Close']
# Calculate daily yield
sp500_returns = sp500_close.pct_change().dropna()
# Calculate the expected annual market return.
Rm = sp500_returns.mean().iloc[0] * 252  # is the number of trading days/year
print(f"Expected market return (annualized) = {Rm:.2%}")

























[*********************100%***********************]  1 of 1 completed












Expected market return (annualized) = 13.85%

































In [13]:






# Calculate ROE using CAPM
risk_free_rate = 0.0269
market_return = 0.1385
roe_capm = risk_free_rate + beta * (market_return - risk_free_rate)
print(f"ROE using CAPM = {roe_capm:.2%}")

























ROE using CAPM = 16.16%

























3. Free Cash Flow (FCF)¶
Free Cash Flow (FCF) represents the cash a company generates from its operations after accounting for capital expenditures. It indicates the cash available to reinvest in the business, pay dividends, reduce debt, or pursue growth opportunities.


The chart below shows Apple Inc.’s Free Cash Flow (FCF) over the most recent five fiscal years, highlighting the company’s strong cash-generating capability and financial sustainability.


Apple’s Free Cash Flow over the past five years demonstrates strong and stable cash generation, supporting sustainable dividends, share buybacks, and strategic investments.


















In [14]:






# Step 1: Get cashflow data

ticker = yf.Ticker("AAPL")
cashflow = ticker.cashflow.T  # transpose year into index

# Step 2: Get Free Cash Flow (FCF)
fcf = cashflow['Free Cash Flow']

# Convert index into year (int)
fcf.index = fcf.index.year

# Step 3: Filter 5 latest years
last_5_years = fcf.index.sort_values()[-5:]
fcf_5y = fcf.loc[last_5_years]

# Step 4: Convert to Million USD
fcf_5y_million = fcf_5y / 1e6

# Step 5: Print for check
print("FCF 5 years (Million USD):")
print(fcf_5y_million)

# Step 6: Plot chart
plt.figure(figsize=(9,5))
plt.plot(fcf_5y_million.index, fcf_5y_million.values, marker='o', color='royalblue', linewidth=2)

# Highlight newest year
plt.scatter(fcf_5y_million.index.max(), fcf_5y_million.values[-1], color='red', zorder=5)
plt.text(fcf_5y_million.index.max(), fcf_5y_million.values[-1]+2000,  # offset để không chạm marker
         f"{fcf_5y_million.values[-1]:,.0f}M", ha='center', color='red', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')

# Title, labels, grid
plt.title(f"Apple Free Cash Flow (FCF) {fcf_5y_million.index.min()}–{fcf_5y_million.index.max()}",
          fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel("Year", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("FCF (Million USD)", fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

# Step 7: Adjust Y-axis for wider range
y_min = fcf_5y_million.min() * 0.95  # 5% below min
y_max = fcf_5y_million.max() * 1.05  # 5% above max
plt.ylim(y_min, y_max)

plt.show()

2022    111443.0

4. Valuation (WACC & Intrinsic Value): WACC Sensitivity Analysis – Intrinsic Value per Share¶






















This table shows how Apple’s intrinsic value per share changes under different WACC assumptions (6%–20%).


Firm value and shares outstanding are based on data from the "Apple 2023 Annual Report" Excel sheet


Free Cash Flow (FCF) = 98,767 million USD


Shares Outstanding = 15,015 million shares


    

  • Firm Value - Intrinsic Firm Value = Free Cash Flow / WACC
    • 

  • Intrinsic value per share = Firm Value / Shares Outstanding
    • 



It helps evaluate how sensitive the valuation is to changes in WACC.


















In [15]:






# Define WACC range (from 6% to 20%)
wacc_range = np.linspace(0.06, 0.20, 50)

firm_value = 98767
shares_outstanding = 15015

# Calculate estimated firm value and value per share at each WACC level
values = firm_value / wacc_range
per_share = values / shares_outstanding

# Create a well-formatted DataFrame for output
valuation_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'WACC (%)': (wacc_range * 100).round(2),
    'Value per Share ($)': per_share.round(2)
})

# Print the first 10 rows as a preview (can be removed to show the full table)
print(valuation_df.head(10))

























   WACC (%)  Value per Share ($)
0      6.00               109.63
1      6.29               104.65
2      6.57               100.10
3      6.86                95.93
4      7.14                92.09
5      7.43                88.55
6      7.71                85.27
7      8.00                82.22
8      8.29                79.39
9      8.57                76.74

























Intrinsic Value vs. WACC (Chart)¶
This plot visualizes the inverse relationship between WACC and intrinsic value per share.


    

  • As WACC increases, the estimated value per share decreases.
    • 

  • Shows the impact of discount rate changes on valuation outcomes.
    • 



This chart makes it easier to interpret the sensitivity table visually.


















In [16]:






import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(wacc_range * 100, per_share, marker='o')
plt.title("Intrinsic Value per Share vs WACC")
plt.xlabel("WACC (%)")
plt.ylabel("Estimated Value per Share (USD)")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
















































5. Monte Carlo Simulation - 100 Simulations for Next 30 Days - Present graphical output - Interpret mean forecast and risk range¶






















This part applies Monte Carlo simulation to estimate the possible future paths of Apple (AAPL) stock price over the next 30 trading days.


    

  • The simulation is based on historical daily returns, using the mean (mu) and standard deviation (sigma) of Apple’s stock returns.
    • 

  • A total of 100 simulations are generated to reflect the potential variability in price movement under a random walk model.
    • 

  • This helps visualize a range of possible outcomes, reflecting market volatility and uncertainty, useful for risk analysis and portfolio planning.
    • 



















In [17]:






days = 30
simulations = 100
last_price = apple_close.iloc[-1]
returns = apple_close.pct_change().dropna()
mu = returns.mean()
sigma = returns.std()

simulations_dict = {}
for i in range(simulations):
    prices = [last_price]
    for _ in range(days):
        shock = np.random.normal(loc=mu, scale=sigma)
        prices.append(prices[-1] * (1 + shock))
    simulations_dict[i] = prices

simulation_df = pd.DataFrame(simulations_dict)

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
plt.plot(simulation_df)
plt.title('Monte Carlo Simulation - Apple Stock Price (30 Days)')
plt.xlabel('Day')
plt.ylabel('Price')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
















































6.FB Prophet Forecast – Apple Stock (Next 30 Days)¶






















We used Facebook Prophet, a time-series forecasting model developed by Meta, to predict Apple's stock price for the next 30 trading days.


Key points:


    

  • The model uses historical closing prices and captures seasonality and trends automatically.
    • 

  • The shaded area represents the 95% confidence interval for future predictions.
    • 

  • The overall forecast shows a relatively stable price trend, with minor fluctuations, supporting the results from our Monte Carlo simulation.
    • 





Prophet is especially useful for time-series data with strong seasonal effects, although for stock prices (which are noisy), the results should be interpreted with caution.




















In [18]:






#!pip install prophet





















In [19]:






# apple_df_2024_2025 = yf.download('AAPL', '2024-12-31', end='2025-12-31')

# apple_close_2024_2025 = apple_df_2024_2025['Close']





















In [20]:






from prophet import Prophet
import pandas as pd

# Prepare data: rename columns for Prophet format (ds: date, y: value)
df_prophet = apple_close_24_25.reset_index()
df_prophet.columns = ['ds', 'y']  # Prophet requires these specific column names

# Create and fit the model
model = Prophet(daily_seasonality=True)
model.fit(df_prophet)

# Forecast next 30 days
future = model.make_future_dataframe(periods=30)
forecast = model.predict(future)

# Plot the forecast
model.plot(forecast);

























11:38:49 - cmdstanpy - INFO - Chain [1] start processing
11:38:50 - cmdstanpy - INFO - Chain [1] done processing




































Compare with Monte Carlo results


Method


Prophet: Deterministic time-series model → fits non-linear trend + seasonality + automatic changepoints to historical data (mainly 2024–early 2025), capturing regime shifts like peaks and troughs.


Monte Carlo: Stochastic simulation → generates thousands of random price paths (typically via geometric Brownian motion) based on historical mean drift (positive expected return) + volatility from past periods, starting from ~$275.


Results


Prophet: Captures a clear U-shaped pattern — predicts mid-term decline after a peak (~$240–$250 range in Prophet fit → bottom ~$200–$210 by mid-2025), followed by gradual recovery; aligns closely with actual path: AAPL hit all-time high closing ~$285.92 on Dec 2, 2025, then corrected significantly (lows ~$246–$247 in Jan 2026), and currently at $257.46 (Mar 6, 2026 close), showing partial rebound but still below late-2025 peak.


Monte Carlo: Produces a wide fan of short-term paths (30 days) mostly drifting upward or sideways from ~$275 start, with clusters ~$250–$325 and extremes up to ~$350 or down to ~$175–$200; reflects short-term optimism (positive drift) + high uncertainty, but many paths remain elevated compared to the actual prolonged correction.


Accuracy in this case


Prophet: Higher accuracy for medium-term trend → successfully detected and modeled the structural change (2025 peak → 2025–2026 bearish correction and slow recovery), with actual prices staying mostly within or near the confidence band despite the deep dip.


Monte Carlo: Lower accuracy for the observed path → overly optimistic on average due to reliance on long-term positive historical drift; failed to anticipate or weight the multi-month downturn adequately, as most simulated paths avoided the sustained drop to ~$246–$257 range seen in reality.


Bottom line:


Prophet excelled in this scenario because it adapts to detected trend changes and non-stationarity in the data (peak → correction → partial rebound). Monte Carlo shines for illustrating risk ranges, tail events, and probability distributions (e.g., chance of hitting lows), but requires updated/calibrated inputs (lower drift, higher volatility during downturns) to reduce bullish bias in trending-down or sideways markets.






















7. TRADE SIGNALS - MOVING AVERAGE¶
This analysis uses yfinance to obtain Apple’s stock data and applies 20-day and 50-day SMAs to identify trends.


Crossover points generate trading signals: a Golden Cross indicates a potential buy opportunity, while a Death Cross suggests a potential sell signal.


The purpose is to visualize price trends and support short-term trading decisions.


















In [21]:






# 1. Set time period (2 years up to Dec 31, 2025)

start_date = '2023-12-31'
end_date = '2025-12-31'  # end date is exclusive

# 2. Download Apple data
df = yf.download('AAPL', start=start_date, end=end_date)

# Keep only closing price
df = df[['Close']]

# 3. Calculate moving averages

df['SMA_20'] = df['Close'].rolling(window=20).mean()
df['SMA_50'] = df['Close'].rolling(window=50).mean()

# 4. Detect crossover signals

df['Signal'] = 0
df.loc[df['SMA_20'] > df['SMA_50'], 'Signal'] = 1
df.loc[df['SMA_20'] < df['SMA_50'], 'Signal'] = -1

# Find where Signal changes → crossover points
df['Crossover'] = df['Signal'].diff()

# Golden Cross (Buy) = 20 SMA crosses above 50 SMA
golden_cross = df[df['Crossover'] == 2]

# Death Cross (Sell) = 20 SMA crosses below 50 SMA
death_cross = df[df['Crossover'] == -2]

# 5. Plot chart
plt.figure(figsize=(14,7))

# Close and SMAs
plt.plot(df['Close'], label='Close Price', color='black', alpha=0.6)
plt.plot(df['SMA_20'], label='20-day SMA', color='orange')
plt.plot(df['SMA_50'], label='50-day SMA', color='green')

# Buy signals (Blue triangle at SMA intersection)
plt.scatter(golden_cross.index, (golden_cross['SMA_20'] + golden_cross['SMA_50'])/2,
            color='blue', marker='^', s=120, label='Buy Signal (Golden Cross)')

# Sell signals (Red triangle at SMA intersection)
plt.scatter(death_cross.index, (death_cross['SMA_20'] + death_cross['SMA_50'])/2,
            color='red', marker='v', s=120, label='Sell Signal (Death Cross)')

# Titles and labels
plt.title('AAPL Moving Averages (20 SMA & 50 SMA) with Buy/Sell Signals', fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel('Date', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Price (USD)', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

























[*********************100%***********************]  1 of 1 completed




































    

  • Uptrend: When the 20-day SMA (orange) is above the 50-day SMA (green) → price tends to rise.
    • 

  • Downtrend: When the 20-day SMA is below the 50-day SMA → price tends to fall.
    • 

  • Golden Cross (▲ blue): Point where the 20-day SMA crosses above the 50-day SMA → potential buy signal.
    • 

  • Death Cross (▼ red): Point where the 20-day SMA crosses below the 50-day SMA → potential sell signal.
    • 



























Date (approx)
Signal
Insight






2024-05
Buy ▲
Uptrend begins → consider buying the stock




2024-08
Sell ▼
SMA 20 crosses down → trend reversal → consider selling to take profit




2024-12
Buy ▲
Price recovers, new uptrend → buy again




2025-03
Sell ▼
Short-term downtrend → consider selling




2025-06
Buy ▲
Uptrend returns → buying opportunity




2025-07
Sell ▼
SMA 20 crosses down → minor correction → consider taking profit




2025-08/09
Buy ▲
Strong uptrend → continue holding or buy more


























MARKET SHARE CHARTS for the Final Report¶


















In [22]:






import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

brands = ["Apple", "Samsung", "Xiaomi", "Others"]
shares = [20.0, 19.1, 13.1, 47.8]

colors = ["#1f77b4", "#ff6f61", "#ffbb78", "#d3d3d3"]

plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))

wedges, texts, autotexts = plt.pie(
    shares,
    labels=brands,
    colors=colors,
    autopct="%1.1f%%",
    startangle=140
)

# Tăng size label (Apple, Samsung...)
for text in texts:
    text.set_fontsize(12)

# Tăng size % (autopct)
for autotext in autotexts:
    autotext.set_fontsize(16)  # <-- chỉnh ở đây (16–20 là đẹp)
    autotext.set_weight("bold")

plt.title("Approximate Global Smartphone Market Share — 2025 (IDC & Counterpoint)", fontsize=14)

plt.show()












































In [23]:






import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Approximate premium share data (H1 2025)
brands = ["Apple (iPhone)", "Samsung", "Other Premium Brands"]
shares = [62, 15, 23]

colors = ["#1f77b4", "#ff6f61", "#d3d3d3"]

plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))

wedges, texts, autotexts = plt.pie(
    shares,
    labels=brands,
    colors=colors,
    autopct="%1.1f%%",
    startangle=140
)

# Tăng size label
for text in texts:
    text.set_fontsize(13)

# Tăng size % (to + đậm cho dễ nhìn)
for autotext in autotexts:
    autotext.set_fontsize(18)
    autotext.set_weight("bold")

plt.title(
    "Approximate Global Premium Smartphone Market Share — H1 2025\n(Apple Dominance)",
    fontsize=15
)

plt.show()
